content:
**Experiment tracking** emerged as ML teams ran hundreds of unlabeled training scripts on shared clusters. Manual notes and scattered checkpoints could not answer which hyperparameters produced a reported benchmark.

**MLflow** (open-sourced by **Databricks**, **2018**) standardized a **tracking API**, **artifact store**, and later **Model Registry** — local `mlruns` folders made tracking accessible without a cloud account.

**Weights & Biases** (**2017**, founders from OpenAI/Uber) targeted **live metric streaming** and **team dashboards**, popularizing collaborative experiment review for deep learning workloads.

Before dedicated tools, teams abused **TensorBoard** logs, **spreadsheet** grids, or **ad-hoc JSON** in `outputs/`. Those approaches lacked **run IDs**, **param search**, and **artifact lineage** at scale.

Today MLflow and W&B sit beside **Neptune**, **Comet**, and cloud-native trackers, but remain the default teaching pair: MLflow for **OSS/registry** patterns, W&B for **visual comparison** and **sweeps** — both implementing the versioning and metrics concepts defined in sibling topics.

timeline.label: Pre-tracking era (pre-2015)
timeline.description: TensorBoard and custom log files capture scalars but not structured params, artifact lineage, or cross-run search.

timeline.label: W&B launch (2017)
timeline.description: Cloud experiment projects with live charts and team sharing for deep learning training loops.

timeline.label: MLflow Tracking (2018)
timeline.description: Open-source run logging, local mlruns store, and unified APIs adopted in Databricks and OSS MLOps stacks.

timeline.label: Registry + CI integration (2020s)
timeline.description: Model Registry, artifact URIs, and tracking hooks in CI/CD become standard for promoted models tied to run IDs.
